questions: patchRegistry currently preserves `current.resolvedConfig.clips` (the resolved clips from the existing data) and only updates the registry entries. After routing through the assembler, `configToRows` will rebuild rows/meta/effects from scratch based on `nextConfig`. Is it acceptable that clip ordering and derived row data might shift slightly, or does patchRegistry need to preserve exact row identity across the patch?

The test helper `makeTimelineData` in useTimelineHistory.test.ts constructs TimelineData with minimal/mock data. Should it use the real `assembleTimelineData` (tighter coupling to implementation) or remain a standalone mock factory (looser coupling but won't validate the assembler)?

Are there any other consumers of TimelineData outside the video-editor tool (e.g., edge functions, workers) that might construct the object independently? The audit only found browser-side construction but server-side code wasn't checked.
success_criteria: Only one function (`assembleTimelineData`) stamps out TimelineData objects — all other construction paths delegate to it
No TimelineData object literal `{ config: ..., rows: ..., ... }` exists outside `assembleTimelineData` (except test mocks if deliberately excluded)
`migrateToFlatTracks` is called exactly once per construction path (no double-migration)
All existing video-editor tests pass without modification (other than test helper updates)
No duplicate-key React warnings when loading V3 timeline data
No cascading `-dup-` clip ID suffixes after registry patches
assumptions: Migration (`migrateToFlatTracks`) is idempotent — running it twice on already-migrated data produces identical output. This is assumed true based on the implementation (dedup is stable, track/clip transforms are no-ops on clean data) and is what makes removing the double-migration safe.

The `patchRegistry` path can safely rebuild rows/meta/effects from config via `configToRows` rather than preserving them from `current`. This is what it already does today (line 329 calls `configToRows(nextConfig)`).
TimelineData is only constructed in browser-side code within the video-editor tool. No edge functions or workers build TimelineData objects.

The `getUniqueClipId` fallback inside `configToRows` (line 177) is kept as defense-in-depth even after migration handles dedup — it logs a warning if it ever fires, which means migration missed something.

Test helpers may optionally continue to construct TimelineData manually if routing through the real assembler would make tests too brittle or slow. The key invariant is that production code uses the assembler.
